coredata.dc:title: Construction of biomass carbon dots@molecularly imprinted polymer fluorescent sensor array for accurate identification of 5-nitroimidazole antibiotics
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Qijun
Fu, Lixing
Yin, Chenhui
Wu, Meng
Liu, Haochi
Niu, Na
Chen, Ligang
coredata.dc:description: The fluorescence sensor array platform is a good choice for detection of 5-Nitroimidazole antibiotics (5-NDZs) in river water samples. However, worrisome selectivity issues inherent in fluorescent sensing units limit their general use in complex samples. In order to solve this problem, we prepared biomass carbon dots based on the catalpa walnut shells by hydrothermal method and formed molecularly imprinted polymers on the surface of carbon dots (CDs@MIPs) by sol-gel process. The cavities of CDs@MIPs enable the exposed binding sites to specifically capture the template molecules, thereby greatly improving the selectivity for 5-NDZs molecules. Different identification materials interacting to varying degrees with 5-NDZs were used to distinguish metronidazole, ornidazole, tinidazole, and secnidazole. The fingerprints were constructed with the information of fluorescence variation degree and the signals were analyzed by linear discriminant analysis. This fluorescence sensor array can sensitively identify these 5-NDZs in a wide range (20–5000 nM). In differential testing, the array platform achieved 100% recognition accuracy for 5-NDZs in buffer solutions and spiked water samples. This protocol not only provided an accurate and simple method for the detection of 5-NDZs in river water samples, but also made a positive contribution to environmental analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor array
Biomass carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
5-Nitroimidazole antibiotics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence test of 5-NDZs based on CDs@MIPs
Fluorescence detection of river water samples based on CDs@MIPs
Construction of the fluorescence sensors array
Characterization of CDs, CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP
Optimization of the conditions of the system for detecting 5-NDZs
Research on the recognition ability of CDs@MIPs
Study on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching
Application of fluorescent sensor array in river water samples detection

R. China (No. TJS202102 ), 111 Project ( B20088 ), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM images of CDs. TEM images of (B) CDs@MIPM, (C) CDs@MIPT and (D) CDs@NIP. (E) UV-Vis spectra of CDs, CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP. (F) XRD spectra of CDs, CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP. (G) FT-IR spectra of CDs, CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP. (H) XPS full spectrum of CDs. High resolution XPS spectra of (I) C 1 s, (J) O 1 s and (K) N 1 s of the CDs. (L) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence quenching curves of (A) CDs@MIP, (B) CDs@MIPT and (C) CDs@NIP after the addition of MTZ. Fluorescence quenching curves of (D) CDs@MIPM, (E) CDs@MIPT and (F) CDs@NIP after the addition of ONZ. Fluorescence quenching curves of (G) CDs@MIPM, (H) CDs@MIPT and (I) CDs@NIP after the addition of TNZ. Fluorescence quenching curves of (J) CDs@MIPM, (K) CDs@MIPT and (L) CDs@NIP after the addition of SEC.

UV–vis spectra of (A) CDs@MIPM, (B) CDs@MIPT and (C) CDs@NIP before and after the addition of MTZ. UV–vis spectra of (D) CDs@MIPM, (E) CDs@MIPT and (F) CDs@NIP before and after the addition of ONZ. UV–vis spectra of (G) CDs@MIPM, (H) CDs@MIPT and (I) CDs@NIP before and after the addition of TNZ. UV–vis spectra of (J) CDs@MIPM, (K) CDs@MIPT and (L) CDs@NIP before and after the addition of SEC.
LDA maps of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP after adding (A) 5000, (B) 3500, (C) 2500, (D) 1500, (E) 800 and (F) 20 nM MTZ, ONZ, TNZ and SEC. Circled areas represent 95% confidence intervals.
(A) Fingerprint of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP. (B) LDA map after adding MTZ, ONZ, TNZ and SEC to river water. Circled areas represent 95% confidence intervals.
Construction strategy of fluorescent sensor array and application to recognition of 5-NDZs.
Comparison of methods for the detection of 5-NDZs.

head.ce:title: Construction of biomass carbon dots@molecularly imprinted polymer fluorescent sensor array for accurate identification of 5-nitroimidazole antibiotics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, College of Food Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, College of Food Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, College of Food Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin, 300457, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence sensor array platform is a good choice for detection of 5-Nitroimidazole antibiotics (5-NDZs) in river water samples. However, worrisome selectivity issues inherent in fluorescent sensing units limit their general use in complex samples. In order to solve this problem, we prepared biomass carbon dots based on the catalpa walnut shells by hydrothermal method and formed molecularly imprinted polymers on the surface of carbon dots (CDs@MIPs) by sol-gel process. The cavities of CDs@MIPs enable the exposed binding sites to specifically capture the template molecules, thereby greatly improving the selectivity for 5-NDZs molecules. Different identification materials interacting to varying degrees with 5-NDZs were used to distinguish metronidazole, ornidazole, tinidazole, and secnidazole. The fingerprints were constructed with the information of fluorescence variation degree and the signals were analyzed by linear discriminant analysis. This fluorescence sensor array can sensitively identify these 5-NDZs in a wide range (20–5000 nM). In differential testing, the array platform achieved 100% recognition accuracy for 5-NDZs in buffer solutions and spiked water samples. This protocol not only provided an accurate and simple method for the detection of 5-NDZs in river water samples, but also made a positive contribution to environmental analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: 5-Nitroimidazole antibiotics (5-NDZs) play an important role in the treatment of Helicobacter pylori infection, amebiasis and other related diseases caused by bacteria or protists [1]. They are also often used to regulate livestock reproduction and increase the productivity of animal husbandry [2]. However, with the extensive use of 5-NDZs, residues are produced in food and water environments through bioconcentration. Due to the complex chemical structures of 5-NDZs, water treatment systems cannot effectively remove them [3]. Their persistence in urban water may affect antibiotic resistance in the general population and even induce neuritis, immune hepatitis, and shock [4]. Currently, many detection methods have been developed for the quantification of 5-NDZs, such as electrochemical method, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and colorimetric method [5–7]. However, these methods have disadvantages that cannot be ignored, such as poor reproducibility, complex sample pretreatment process, and high limit of detection (LOD). In contrast, fluorescence method has the advantages of simplicity, rapidity and sensitivity as a good choice for the detection of 5-NDZs [8]. Moreover, there are concerns about the specific recognition ability of a single fluorescent sensor when detecting multiple targets, so it is crucial to develop a sensing platform that can specifically distinguish different kinds of 5-NDZs in complex samples.

Fluorescent sensor arrays are sensing systems composed of multiple fluorescent sensor elements that generate unique response patterns for each analyte and associated event [9]. The response of different sensing units to the same analyte in an "array" system will produce different results, and the same sensing unit will also produce different responses to different analytes. The response signals are analyzed by a series of supervised methods such as hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), and principal component analysis (PCA). The system projects high-dimensional data into the low-dimensional space while retaining as much class discrimination information as possible to maximize the ratio of between-class variance to within-class variance, thereby realizing the differentiation of different signals [10]. Sensing platforms based on fluorescent sensor arrays have enabled the detection of various targets, such as heavy metal ions, disease markers, and drugs [11–13]. Zhao et al. report an array of 36 groups of conjugated polymer nanoparticles for the identification of 30 volatile compounds [14]. Zhang et al. designed a gold nanocluster-based fluorescence sensors array to realize the detection of different heavy metal ions [15]. Although fluorescent sensor arrays have significant advantages in related fields, there are still problems that cannot be ignored in their wider applications, such as the fluorescence properties of probes and the selectivity of a single sensing unit. Specifically, most fluorescent probes detect analytes based on the fluorescence on or off mode, and their sensing sensitivity mainly depends on the fluorescence properties of the probes [16]. However, when faced with complex matrix samples, the anti-interference ability of a single probe is slightly insufficient [17]. In this context, the construction of composites that improve probe selectivity is as important as the design of probes with high fluorescence performance. Therefore, it remains attractive to develop low toxicity, low cost and high-performance fluorescent probes that can be adapted to fluorescent sensor arrays, as well as design strategies to improve their selectivity.

Recently, our team had developed a bone-based carbon dots fluorescent sensor array for heavy metal ion detection [18]. On this basis, to further improve the performance of the sensing units, we established a fluorescence sensor array based on biomass carbon dots@molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs@MIPs) for selective identification of 5-NDZ in river water samples. Specifically, CDs were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis method using the cheap biomass waste walnut shell as the carbon source. The inherent lignin, cellulose and other substances in the walnut shell play a positive role in the self-modification during the formation of CDs. Then, the molecularly imprinted layer was formed on the surface of CDs by sol-gel process using metronidazole (MTZ) and tinidazole (TNZ) as template molecules, respectively. The prepared CDs@MIPs formed special connections with MTZ, TNZ and their structural analogs ornidazole (ONZ) and secnidazole (SEC) through the cavity of the template molecules, resulting in different degrees of fluorescence quenching of the polymers. Finally, the responses of the sensor array were analyzed by LDA, thus realizing the sensitive and selective detection of MTZ, ONZ, TNZ and SEC in river water samples ( Scheme 1).

The catalpa walnut shells were made into powder with the high-speed pulverizer. Walnut catalpa shell powder (2.0 g) was added to 30 mL of ultrapure water and dispersed evenly. The mixed solution was placed in a stainless steel reactor lined with PTFE and hydrothermally reacted at 200 °C for 10 h. The product liquid was filtered through filter membranes with the pore size of 0.22 µm and then dialyzed (3500 Da) for 48 h. The dialysate was freeze-dried to obtain brown CDs.

CDs@MIPM were synthesized by the sol-gel process. MTZ (1 mmol), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) (4 mmol), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) (20 mmol), H2O (20 mL), CDs (100 mg), NH3·H2O (25% wt) (0.3 mL) were mixed and stirred in the dark for 24 h. After that, the product was centrifuged for 10 min and the supernatant was discarded. Afterwards, the remaining solid was subjected to Soxhlet extraction to remove template molecules. The product was dried under vacuum at 60 °C to obtain CDs@MIPM. The synthesis process of CDs@MIPT was the same as above, except that TNZ was used instead of MTZ. Non-molecularly imprinted polymers (CDs@NIP) were prepared in the same way as CDs@MIPM, except that MTZ was not added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence test of 5-NDZs based on CDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs@MIPs powder (25 mg) was dispersed in Tris-HCl buffer solution (30 mL). Afterwards, a series of 5-NDZs solutions were added to the mixed system with the volume ratio of 1:1. The concentrations of 5-NDZs were 20, 800, 1500, 2500, 3500 and 5000 nM, respectively. The system was mixed evenly and incubated for 5 min, and the fluorescence spectrum analysis was performed immediately with an excitation wavelength of 329 nm. F and F0 were recorded as the fluorescence intensities of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP in the presence and absence of 5-NDZs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of river water samples based on CDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Impurities in the river water may cause false positive responses in the detection system, so the water samples were first filtered with 0.22 µm membranes. MTZ, ONZ, TNZ and SEC solutions prepared in ambient water were added to CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP, respectively. After incubation for 5 min, the change in fluorescence intensity was recorded. The test conditions are the same as 2.6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the fluorescence sensors array
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor array is constructed using fingerprint patterns. Each detection step was repeated 5 times to generate CDs@MIPM, CDs@MIPT and CDs@NIP × 4 5-NDZs × 5 repeated training matrices.
Other methods details are described in Supplementary Material S1.

The synthesis of CDs is a process of converting cheap catalpa walnut shells into high-performance fluorescent sensing materials, which realizes the secondary utilization of waste. The main components of catalpa walnut shell are lignin and cellulose and a small amount of protein and vitamins. In the hydrothermal reaction, these substances will undergo hydrolysis, dehydration and other processes to generate soluble compounds such as acid and furfural [19]. Biomass CDs are prepared by nuclear cleavage of these compounds at the critical concentration of the supersaturation point. During the synthesis process, the inherent hydrophilic groups such as -OH and -COOH in the catalpa walnut shell are modified to the surface of the CDs, which is positive for improving the water dispersibility and enhancing the fluorescence properties of the CDs [20]. The temperature and duration of the hydrothermal reaction are also key factors determining the fluorescence properties of CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased with the temperature or time of the hydrothermal reaction. However, when the temperature was increased to 200 °C and the reaction time reached 10 h, continuing to increase the reaction temperature or prolong the holding time could not effectively improve the fluorescence properties of CDs.

However, CDs are often able to respond to 5-NDZs, and the responses are even more indistinguishable when confronted with structural analogs. To address this issue, molecularly imprinted polymers are used as sensitive layers to enable discriminative detection of structural analogs. Fig. S2 shows the molecular structures of MTZ, ONZ, TNZ, SEC and their binding forms to CD@MIPs. Under the catalysis of NH3·H2O, the alkoxysilane groups in template molecules, APTES, and TEOS undergo hydrolysis and crosslinking, so that CDs are incorporated into the Si-O-Si backbone network to form CD@MIPs. After elution of template molecules, the surfaces of CDs@MIPs have cavities with similar spatial size and shape to those of MTZ and TNZ, respectively. Based on the fact that the structures of different species of 5-NDZs are not identical, resulting in different degrees of filling of 5-NDZs into CDs@MIPs. Since 5-NDZs are effective electron acceptors, the addition and removal of 5-NDZs are related to the filling and removal of electron holes at the interface of CDs@MIPs composites, which in turn can achieve fluorescence recovery and quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the four materials were observed by TEM. The particle size distribution of CDs is calculated to be 3–5 nm and the average diameter is 4.04 nm (Fig. S3). After testing, the zeta potential of CDs dispersed in water is almost neutral (Fig. S4). HRTEM shows that the CDs have clear lattice fringes with the spacing of about 0.06 nm. The average diameters of the synthesized CDs@MIPM, CDs@MIPT, and CDs@NIP are about 20–40 nm ( Fig. 1B-D). The size of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP is significantly larger than that of CDs, indicating that CDs have been completely encapsulated by the molecular imprinting layer.

Due to the n-π * and π-π * transitions in the polyconjugated structures of CO and C-O inside the CDs and their composites, their UV-Vis spectra show two characteristic peaks at 270 nm and 340 nm (Fig. 1E) [21].

The crystal structure of materials were further examined by XRD. Fig. 1 F shows that the spectra of the four materials all have broad diffraction peaks at 15°− 35° without additional impurity peaks. This peak corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, indicating that both the CDs and the composites maintain the state of amorphous carbon [22].

The FT-IR spectra of CDs showed the bonding form of each group (Fig. 1 G). There are obvious characteristic absorption peaks at 1430 cm−1, 1130 cm−1, 1560 cm−1 and 3450 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum. These peaks consist of bending vibrations of C-H and stretching vibrations of C-O, CO, and O-H, respectively. The FT-IR spectra of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP verified their successful preparation. Besides the vibrational peaks at 1300 cm−1 of the benzene skeleton, there are also have C-N stretching vibration peaks at 1590 cm−1. The characteristic peaks at 777 cm−1, 1074 cm−1, 2949 cm−1 and 3451 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of Si-O, Si-O-Si, C-H and N-H, respectively.

XPS spectra are used to interpret the presence of elements in CDs, CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP. Fig. 1H shows that CDs are composed of C, N and O elements with contents of 39.73%, 58.59% and 1.68%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s revealed four peaks at 283.9, 284.4, 284.8, and 285.2 eV, belonging to C-C/CC, C-N, C-O, and C-OC (Fig. 1I). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s consists of two peaks centered at 531.4 and 532.3 eV, belonging to CO and C-OH/C-O-C (Fig. 1 J). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s revealed two characteristic peaks at 397.1 and 399.8 eV, belonging to C-N-C and N-H (Fig. 1 K). This proves that the surface of CDs has functional groups such as -COOH, -OH, -NH2, which ensure their good water dispersibility. The XPS spectra of CDs@MIPM, CDs@MIPT, and CDs@NIP showed that the three polymers are composed of C, N, O, and Si (Fig. S5-Fig. S7, Table. S1). Compared with the XPS spectra of CDs, CDs@MIPT significantly increased two characteristic peaks of Si (Fig. S6A). In addition, the peaks of C, N, and O are red-shifted due to the influence of Si.

By exploring the fluorescence properties of CDs, it is found that the emission peaks of CDs remained almost unchanged with the change of excitation wavelength (Fig. 1 L). This indicates that the photoluminescence properties of CDs are independent of excitation, which may be due to surface defects [23]. The fluorescence intensity exhibited by the CDs reaches the maximum when the excitation wavelength is 329 nm. Quinine sulfate was used as the reference, the quantum yield (QY) of CDs was 10.2%, and the QY standard deviation of five batches of CDs was 0.81%. Furthermore, studies on the fluorescence properties of CDs dispersed in different solvents showed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs dispersed in polar protic solvents (N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF)) was higher than that in polar aprotic solvents (water, ethanol, methanol) (Fig. S8). We speculate that hydrogen bonds between CDs and polar protic solvents cause changes in fluorescence intensity [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the conditions of the system for detecting 5-NDZs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the system is also an important factor in the detection efficiency because the target molecule will have different ionic forms in different pH ranges. Fig. S9 shows that the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP is not greatly affected by pH, but the fluorescence intensity decreases to a certain extent in the case of strong acid and strong base, indicating that the inner CDs are well protected by the polymer layer. After the addition of 5-NDZs, the quenching degree of the sensor in pH> 9.0 and pH< 6.0 media due to the reduced interaction between the vacancy sites in strongly basic media and 5-NDZs molecules and the interference of hydrogen bonds in strongly acidic media reduce [25]. Under strongly acidic conditions, protonation of the recognition site weakened the interaction between 5-NDZs and CDs@MIPs. The deprotonation of the -NH2 group of APTES at pH 3.0–8.0 enhances the electrostatic repulsion between the 5-NDZs and CDs@MIPs, thereby enhancing the fluorescence quenching efficiency. The slight change may be due to the deprotonation of -NH3 groups equilibrating the -COOH groups [26]. When pH> 9.0, the degree of fluorescence quenching decreases due to surface defects caused by the ionization of the silica layer. Overall, the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP peaked at pH 8. Therefore, this work chooses to operate in the environment of pH = 8.

In addition, the incubation time of the system was further optimized. As shown in Fig. S10, the quenching time of the four nitroimidazole antibiotics to CDs@MIPs is shorter than that of CDs@NIP, indicating that CDs@MIPs have sites and holes that can bind nitroimidazole antibiotics, which improves the response efficiency. The response rates of MTZ to CDs@MIPM and TNZ to CDs@MIPT are much higher than other systems, which also shows the excellent recognition ability of the prepared molecularly imprinted polymers for their template molecules. In general, all systems can complete the response of the fluorescent signal within 5 min. Therefore, the incubation time of the system was set to 5 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on the recognition ability of CDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para:
To demonstrate the ability of CDs@MIPs to recognize 5-NDZs, the fluorescence spectral responses of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP were tested after adding different concentrations of 5-NDZs. When the concentrations of MTZ and TNZ in the system continued to increase, the degree of fluorescence quenching of CDs@MIPs gradually increased and showed a good linear relationship ( Fig. 2). Meanwhile, ONZ and SEC, which are used as structural analogs of MTZ and TNZ, also have a quenching effect on CDs@MIPs. As the molecularly imprinted polymer elutes the cavity of the template molecule, the exposed binding sites can specifically capture the template molecule. In the recognition cavity of CDs@MIPs, intermolecular hydrogen bonds are formed between the -NH2 of APTES and the template molecule thus enhancing the binding efficiency [27]. These processes all fit the the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. 1).
(1)
F
0
F
=
K
SV
Q+ 1
where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity with and without the addition of the test substances, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant and Q is the added concentration of the test substances. CDs@MIPM and CDs@MIPT established good linear relationships at the concentration of 5-NDZs from 20 to 5000 nM.

Imprinting factor (IF = KsvCDsMIPs/KsvCDsNIP) is used to evaluate the selectivity of materials. The IF of CDs@MIPM and CDs@MIPT in this work were calculated to be 4.25 and 2.55, respectively, according to the following linear equations (Eqs. 2–5). It is indicated that CDs@MIPs have better ability to recognize template molecules than CDs@NIP. CDs@MIPs can improve the selectivity of MTZ and TNZ during the detection process.
(2)
F
0 /F −1 = 1·86·10−3 Q + 0.2152 (CDs@MIPM) R2 = 0.9949
(3)
F
0 /F −1 = 4·38·10−4 Q + 0.1496 (CDs@NIP) R2 = 0.9923
(4)
F
0 /F −1 = 6·53·10−4 Q + 0.0653 (CDs@MIPT) R2 = 0.9928
(5)
F
0 /F −1 = 2·56·10−4 Q + 0.6134 (CDs@NIP) R2 = 0.9924

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: Static quenching, dynamic quenching or a combination of the two are several common fluorescence quenching mechanisms. Judging whether the mechanism is single dynamic quenching or static quenching is based on whether the fluorescence intensity changes linearly with the concentration of the quencher [28]. When the curve fitting is nonlinear, the fluorescence quenching mechanism belongs to the combination of the two, otherwise it is a single quenching form. Fig. 2 shows that the fluorescence intensity (F0/F) is linear with the quencher concentration (Q), and the Ksv constants are greater than 100 L·mol−1. Therefore, the quenching mechanism is considered to be static. The static quenching mechanism was further verified by UV-vis spectra. As shown in Fig. 3, the characteristic UV absorption peaks of 5-NDZs are around 231 nm and 320 nm. After mixing with CDs@MIPs, the UV absorption peaks of the 5-NDZs shift to 228 nm and 316 nm. The results indicate that the two interact to form a ground-state complex, which is consistent with the static quenching properties.

The CDs@MIPM, CDs@MIPT and CDs@NIP solutions were mixed with different concentrations of MTZ, ONZ, TNZ and SEC solutions, and the measurement was repeated 5 times. A multidimensional response pattern of 3 sensing units × 4 5-NDZs × 5 repeats was obtained. Fig. S11 shows the fluorescence quenching relationship of different sensing units caused by different concentrations of antibiotics. The fingerprint of each 5-NDZs was generated according to the change of fluorescence intensity. Once the binding properties of the polymers to the four imidazoles were determined, sensor array consisting of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP as 5-NDZs sensing units could be constructed.

Since the fluorescence quenching signals of the same sensing units for different targets are different and the fluorescence quenching signals of different sensing units for the same targets are also different. Therefore, LDA is adopted to project high-dimensional pattern samples into the optimal discriminant vector space to maximize the variance ratio of events, so as to achieve maximum data separation and the effect of extracting classification information and compressing the dimension of feature space [9]. By reducing the training matrix size and converting it to canonical factors, a series of well-clustered 2D canonical score plots were obtained ( Fig. 4). The 20 training datasets are divided into 4 groups, and each point represents the response of the sensor array to a single sample. The four 5-NDZs at 5000 nM were completely separated, and the identification accuracy was 100%. The first two typical factors contributed 99.6% of the total variance (factor 1 = 92.3%, factor 2 = 7.3%). At other concentrations, the sensor array can also achieve accurate identification of different classes of 5-NDZs with 100% accuracy. This strategy can detect 5-NDZs at 20 nM under the premise of satisfying the necessary classification and identification accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of fluorescent sensor array in river water samples detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the constructed fluorescent array sensing platform was tested before the detection of river water samples. Common ions and organic compounds in river water were used as interferents at 30 times the concentration of 5-NDZs. The result shows that the interfering substances did not significantly affect the fluorescence signals of the sensing units (Fig. S12).

To demonstrate that the constructed sensing platform has stable CDs suitable for real sample detection, the fluorescence properties of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP after long-term storage were investigated. The powdered CDs, CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP were stored at room temperature for a long time and their fluorescence properties were tested every two days. As shown in Fig. S13, the fluorescence intensity of the four materials did not change significantly over ten days, which confirms their good stability.

According to the National Standard of the People's Republic of China (GB 31650–2019), European Council Regulation No. 613/98/EEC and relevant regulations of the U.S. Food and Drug Administration, metronidazole and other nitroimidazole antibiotics are prohibited in animal breeding, aquaculture and food [29–31]. So literally, food and environmental water should not contain residues of nitroimidazole antibiotics. River water samples were first subjected to HPLC testing to check the 5-NDZs content. The result is that there are no obvious signals for MTZ, ONZ, TNZ and SEC in the river water samples. Therefore, the standard addition method was used to test the river water samples. The solutions of MTZ, ONZ, TNZ, and SEC were added to the river water samples at the concentration of 600 nM, and the fluorescence fingerprint obtained after testing is shown in Fig. 5 A. For the sensing units of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIP, each 5-NDZs has a different degree of quenching. The LDA map showed that the four imidazoles were clearly divided into four classes (Fig. 5B). The sum of the two factors is 94.3%, indicating that most of the sample information has been included, so it can be used as a basis for representing the overall sample. The experimental results verify the practicability and stability of the fluorescent sensor array, which can accurately identify and classify 5-NDZs.

The advancement and practicability of the method proposed in this work are judged by comparing with the detection results of the previously reported 5-NDZs sensors ( Table 1). Among these, the synthesis of sensors composed of gold nanoparticles and organometallic frameworks is complex and high cost. This work requires only one step to synthesize walnut catalpa shell CDs with low cost and realizes waste utilization. The selectivity of the material is further improved by wrapping a molecularly imprinted polymer layer on the surface of the CDs. In addition, although the linear range of this work is comparable to that of the restricted molecularly imprinted sensor, the simultaneous detection of multiple 5-NDZs is achieved. Therefore, this method has broad prospects in the identification of 5-NDZs.

In conclusion, an efficient and general strategy was proposed to construct CDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor array for highly selective recognition of MTZ, ODZ, TNZ and SCZ. An environmentally friendly hydrothermal synthesis method was adopted in the preparation of the CDs from the walnut shell without the addition of additional chemical reagents. The synthesized CDs@MIPM and CDs@MIPT combined the excellent fluorescence properties of CDs and the advantage of MIPs that can form differential responses to structural analogs. The LDA pattern recognition method was used to distinguish and detect 5-NDZs in a wide concentration range, and the classification accuracy was 100%. In addition, good detection results were obtained by applying the fluorescent sensor array to the identification of 5-NDZs in river water. This work provides a simple and cost-effective method for the detection of 5-NDZs.

Qijun Sun: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.Lixing Fu: Methodology, Supervision, Validation. Chenhui Yin: Methodology. Meng Wu: Validation. Haochi Liu: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Na Niu: Funding acquisition, Validation. Ligang Chen: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572022DJ01), The Project program of Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, P. R. China (No. TJS202102), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132716.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qijun Sun is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His major is chemical biology and his research direction is fluorescence sensing analysis.

Lixing Fu is a master student at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her major is chemistry and his research direction is molecular imprinting techniques.

Chenhui Yin is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His major is chemical biology and his research direction is fluorescence sensing.

Meng Wu is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her major is chemical biology and her research direction is fluorescence imaging.

Haochi Liu is a lecturer at the College of Food Science and Engineering at Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her major is chemistry and her research direction is fluorescence sensing techniques.

Na Niu is an associate professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her main research interests are in multifunctional materials-based bioapplications.

Ligang Chen is a professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His main research directions are biosensing probes and molecular imprinting techniques.
